content.text: DEPRECATED: Azure Blueprints retired July 11, 2026. This topic covers artifact types and parameter binding for teams managing existing blueprint assignments.

Blueprint artifacts are the four deployable components attached to a blueprint definition: resource groups (creates an RG in the target subscription), ARM templates (deploys resources into an RG placeholder), policy assignments (assigns Azure Policy at subscription scope), and role assignments (grants RBAC roles). All four types use the same parameter binding syntax — [parameters('paramName')] — to pull values from blueprint-level parameters. Deployment order follows the dependsOn dependency graph: resource group artifacts deploy first (they have no dependencies), ARM templates deploy after the resource groups they target, and policy and role assignments can deploy in parallel after the RG stage.

attrs.alt: Blueprint artifact types, JSON schema structure, dependency chain, and parameter binding pattern

content.text: Resource group placeholders are defined in the blueprint definition's resourceGroups property, not as separate artifact objects. Each placeholder has a display name, optional tags, and a location that can reference blueprint parameters. At assignment time, the caller maps each placeholder name (rg-workloads, rg-monitoring) to an actual resource group name and location for the target subscription — this allows one blueprint definition to serve subscriptions in different regions or with different naming conventions. ARM template artifacts reference the placeholder name via --resource-group; the actual RG name is resolved at deployment time.

ARM template artifacts deploy resources into a resource group placeholder. The template is embedded inline in the artifact JSON (max 4 MB). For larger templates, use a nested deployment that links to a template stored in Azure Blob Storage. Artifact parameters bridge blueprint-level parameters to ARM template parameters using the [parameters('bpParamName')] expression in the artifact's parameters block — the ARM template itself uses its own parameter names, and the artifact maps blueprint values into them. The --depends-on flag ensures the resource group placeholder exists before the deployment runs.

# ARM Template artifact — deploys resources into a resource group placeholder
# Template must be inline JSON (max 4MB) or linked via storageAccountId.

MG_ID='alz-landing-zones'
BP_NAME='landing-zone-baseline'

# Option A: Inline template (max 4MB — use for simple resources)
az blueprint artifact template create \
  --blueprint-name $BP_NAME \
  --management-group $MG_ID \
  --artifact-name 'key-vault' \
  --display-name 'Key Vault for workload secrets' \
  --resource-group 'rg-workloads' \
  --depends-on 'rg-workloads' \
  --template '{
    "$schema": "https://schema.management.azure.com/schemas/2019-04-01/deploymentTemplate.json#",
    "contentVersion": "1.0.0.0",
    "parameters": {
      "location": {"type": "string"},
      "keyVaultAdminObjectId": {"type": "string"}
    },
    "resources": [{
      "type": "Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults",
      "apiVersion": "2023-02-01",
      "name": "[concat(\"kv-\", uniqueString(resourceGroup().id))]",
      "location": "[parameters(\"location\")]",
      "properties": {
        "sku": {"family": "A", "name": "standard"},
        "tenantId": "[subscription().tenantId]",
        "enableRbacAuthorization": true,
        "enableSoftDelete": true,
        "softDeleteRetentionInDays": 90,
        "enablePurgeProtection": true,
        "networkAcls": {"defaultAction": "Deny", "bypass": "AzureServices"}
      }
    }]
  }' \
  --parameters '{
    "location": {"value": "[parameters(\"subscriptionLocation\")]"},
    "keyVaultAdminObjectId": {"value": "[parameters(\"appTeamGroupObjectId\")]"}'

# Option B: Linked template stored in a Storage Account
# Use when the template exceeds 4MB or contains complex nested deployments.
# The storage account must be accessible from Azure during blueprint assignment.
az blueprint artifact template create \
  --blueprint-name $BP_NAME \
  --management-group $MG_ID \
  --artifact-name 'complex-networking' \
  --resource-group 'rg-workloads' \
  --depends-on 'rg-workloads' \
  --template-file ./large-networking-template.json

Policy assignment artifacts assign Azure Policy definitions at subscription scope when the blueprint is assigned. Audit-effect and Deny-effect policies require no managed identity. DeployIfNotExists (DINE) and Modify-effect policies require a system-assigned identity on the artifact (--identity-type SystemAssigned) — the blueprint service principal uses this identity to deploy resources on non-compliant resources. After blueprint assignment, DINE policies still require a manual remediation task to apply to existing resources; the blueprint assignment does not auto-remediate. The system-assigned identity needs Contributor at subscription scope for the DINE effect to function.

# Policy Assignment artifact — assigns Azure Policy at subscription scope during blueprint assignment
# DINE-effect policies require a system-assigned managed identity on the artifact.

MG_ID='alz-landing-zones'
BP_NAME='landing-zone-baseline'

# Audit-effect policy: no identity needed
az blueprint artifact policy create \
  --blueprint-name $BP_NAME \
  --management-group $MG_ID \
  --artifact-name 'require-https-storage' \
  --display-name 'Storage accounts should use HTTPS transfer' \
  --policy-definition-id '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/404c3081-a854-4457-ae30-26a93ef643f9' \
  --parameters '{}'

# DeployIfNotExists (DINE) policy: system-assigned identity is REQUIRED
# The blueprint service principal needs Contributor at sub scope to create diag settings.
az blueprint artifact policy create \
  --blueprint-name $BP_NAME \
  --management-group $MG_ID \
  --artifact-name 'deploy-diag-storage' \
  --display-name 'Deploy diagnostic settings for Storage Accounts' \
  --policy-definition-id '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/59759c62-9a22-4cdf-ae64-074495983fef' \
  --parameters '{
    "logAnalyticsWorkspace": {
      "value": "[parameters(\"logAnalyticsWorkspaceId\")]"
    }
  }' \
  --identity-type SystemAssigned

# After assigning the blueprint, the DINE policy also needs a remediation task
# (same as any DINE policy — blueprint assignment does not auto-remediate existing resources)
# The blueprint system-assigned identity must have Contributor on the subscription
# for the DINE effect to create diagnostic settings on existing resources.
# Grant this via a role assignment artifact OR manually post-assignment:
az role assignment create \
  --role 'Contributor' \
  --assignee '<blueprint-assignment-principal-id>' \
  --scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>'

Role assignment artifacts grant RBAC roles at subscription scope or at a specific resource group placeholder. The principal can be a hardcoded AAD object ID (group, service principal, managed identity) or a value from a blueprint parameter. Always specify principalType (Group, ServicePrincipal, User) in the JSON — ARM resolves the type via a directory lookup if omitted, which can fail with permission errors in strict tenants. Role assignment artifacts at resource group scope use --resource-group with the placeholder name; the role is scoped to the actual resource group name resolved at assignment.

# Role Assignment artifact — grants RBAC roles at subscription or resource group scope
# Principal IDs can be hardcoded AAD group OIDs or bound to blueprint parameters.

MG_ID='alz-landing-zones'
BP_NAME='landing-zone-baseline'

# Role assignment at subscription scope — Contributor for app team group
# Use principalType 'Group' for AAD groups (avoids lookup delays in ARM)
az blueprint artifact role create \
  --blueprint-name $BP_NAME \
  --management-group $MG_ID \
  --artifact-name 'app-team-contributor' \
  --display-name 'App team Contributor on subscription' \
  --role-definition-id '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions/b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c' \
  --principal-ids '[parameters("appTeamGroupObjectId")]'

# Role assignment at resource group scope (scope the contributor to a single RG)
az blueprint artifact role create \
  --blueprint-name $BP_NAME \
  --management-group $MG_ID \
  --artifact-name 'app-team-contributor-workloads-rg' \
  --display-name 'App team Contributor scoped to workload RG' \
  --role-definition-id '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions/b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c' \
  --principal-ids '[parameters("appTeamGroupObjectId")]' \
  --resource-group 'rg-workloads'

# Built-in role definition IDs commonly used in blueprints:
# Owner:       8e3af657-a8ff-443c-a75c-2fe8c4bcb635
# Contributor: b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c
# Reader:      acdd72a7-3385-48ef-bd42-f606fba81ae7
# User Access Administrator: 18d7d88d-d35e-4fb5-a5c3-7773c20a72d8

# List all artifacts in the blueprint to verify
az blueprint artifact list \
  --blueprint-name $BP_NAME \
  --management-group $MG_ID \
  --query '[].{Name:name, Kind:kind, DependsOn:dependsOn}' -o table

Blueprint artifacts have hard limits: 500 artifacts per blueprint definition, 4 MB per inline ARM template, 100 policy parameters per artifact. When an ARM template exceeds 4 MB, use a nested deployment that references a linked template in Azure Blob Storage — the inline artifact is a thin wrapper that calls a nested Microsoft.Resources/deployments resource pointing to the stored URI. When the 500-artifact ceiling is approached, split the blueprint into two definitions (infrastructure and governance) and assign in order using a pipeline that waits for the first assignment to reach provisioningState = succeeded before starting the second.

# Blueprint artifact limits and workarounds

# ---- Hard limits ----
# Max artifacts per blueprint: 500
# Max inline ARM template size: 4 MB
# Max policy parameters per artifact: 100
# Max parameter length (string): 1 MB
# Max blueprint definition size (all artifacts combined): 2 GB

# ---- Workaround: Linked template for large ARM templates ----
# When inline template exceeds 4 MB, store in Azure Storage and reference it.
# The storage account must allow public blob access OR be accessible to the
# blueprint service during assignment (use SAS token or managed identity access).

# Store large template in storage and get URI
STORAGE_ACCOUNT='stblueprinttemplates'
CONTAINER='templates'
TEMPLATE_FILE='large-workload-template.json'

az storage blob upload \
  --account-name $STORAGE_ACCOUNT \
  --container-name $CONTAINER \
  --name $TEMPLATE_FILE \
  --file $TEMPLATE_FILE

TEMPLATE_URI=$(az storage blob url \
  --account-name $STORAGE_ACCOUNT \
  --container-name $CONTAINER \
  --name $TEMPLATE_FILE -o tsv)

# Reference via nested/linked deployment in the inline template:
# {
#   "resources": [{
#     "type": "Microsoft.Resources/deployments",
#     "properties": {
#       "mode": "Incremental",
#       "templateLink": { "uri": "<TEMPLATE_URI>" }
#     }
#   }]
# }

# ---- Workaround: Chaining blueprints for very large environments ----
# If 500 artifacts is not enough, split into multiple blueprints:
# Blueprint A: resource groups + baseline networking + monitoring
# Blueprint B: policy assignments + role assignments
# Assign in order: A first (wait for Succeeded), then B.
az blueprint assignment show \
  --name 'assign-blueprint-a' \
  --subscription '<sub-id>' \
  --query 'provisioningState' -o tsv
# Proceed with Blueprint B only when output is 'succeeded'
Inline resource group in Bicep template
az policy assignment create in post-stack script
ALZ-Bicep policy module or policy assignment in stack

Blueprint artifacts are the four deployable units inside a blueprint definition: resource group placeholders (defined in the blueprint, resolved to actual RG names at assignment), ARM template artifacts (deploy into RG placeholders with a 4 MB inline limit), policy assignment artifacts (Audit/Deny need no identity; DINE requires system-assigned identity), and role assignment artifacts (subscription or RG scope; always declare principalType). Artifacts reference blueprint-level parameters via [parameters('name')] in their parameters blocks. Deployment order follows the dependsOn dependency graph — resource groups first, then templates, then policies and roles in parallel.
